courses.title: The Art of Active Listening
courses.teaser: Most of us listen to reply. Active listeners do the opposite — and people remember them forever.

courses.title: The Strange Economics of Airfare

courses.title: The Anchoring Effect

courses.title: The Antikythera Mechanism

courses.title: Anxiety vs. Fear

courses.title: Why Apple's Logo Has a Bite
courses.teaser: Is it a tribute to Alan Turing? A nod to Newton? Or just a trick to stop it looking like a cherry?

courses.title: Arranged Marriages vs. Love Marriages: The Data

courses.title: The 1% Better Rule
courses.teaser: Improve by 1% a day for a year and you end up 37 times better. The math is almost unfair.

courses.title: Attachment Theory

courses.title: How the Northern Lights Work

courses.title: How Babies Learn Language

courses.title: Ball Lightning: The Weather We Can't Explain

courses.title: How Deep-Sea Animals Make Their Own Light

courses.title: The Blue Ocean Strategy

courses.title: The Blue Zones: Where People Live Longest

courses.title: The Ancient Art of Bonsai

courses.title: Box Breathing: A Navy SEAL Technique
courses.teaser: The same breathing trick that calms elite operators before a firefight can calm you before a presentation.

courses.title: How Caffeine Hijacks Your Brain
courses.teaser: Your morning coffee doesn't give you energy. It just tricks your brain into forgetting it's tired.

courses.teaser: Why does an American judge read old cases while a French judge reads a code? Two legal worlds, one planet.

courses.title: How Giant Dams Hold Back Rivers

courses.title: The Dancing Plague of 1518

courses.teaser: When a story paints itself into a corner — and a god literally flies in to fix it.

courses.title: You Are Mostly Microbes

courses.title: The 8 Glasses a Day Myth

courses.teaser: Why does a wobbly bookshelf you assembled feel more valuable than a perfect one you bought?

courses.title: Ikigai: The Japanese Reason to Get Up

courses.title: The Immortal Jellyfish

courses.title: The Innovator's Dilemma

courses.title: Why 'Junk DNA' Isn't Junk

courses.title: The Kola Superdeep Borehole

courses.title: The 4:1 Rule for Safe Ladder Use
courses.teaser: One quick ratio keeps your ladder from turning into a catapult. Learn it before you climb one again.

courses.title: Why Leaves Change Color

courses.title: The Library of Alexandria

courses.title: Ancient Life Trapped in Amber

courses.title: The Birth of the Little Black Dress

courses.title: How to Spot a Load-Bearing Wall

courses.title: 5 Logical Fallacies You've Heard This Week

courses.title: Why the Longest Flights Take Weird Routes
courses.teaser: New York to Singapore in a straight line? Not quite — your plane is drawing a curve across the top of the planet.

courses.title: Loving-Kindness Meditation

courses.title: Why Africa Looks Smaller on Most Maps

courses.title: Why Marathons Are Exactly 26.2 Miles

courses.title: The Marshmallow Test, Revisited

courses.title: How Meditation Changes Your Brain
courses.teaser: Eight weeks of sitting still can physically reshape your brain. Here's what the scans show.

courses.title: Why Familiarity Breeds Affection
courses.teaser: You're more likely to fall for the person sitting next to you in class than the most attractive stranger across the country.

courses.title: Method Acting
courses.teaser: Why do some actors live as their characters for months — and does it actually make the performance better?

courses.title: Why Earth Has Ice Ages
courses.teaser: Earth's orbit wobbles like a spinning top. Those wobbles build and melt continent-sized glaciers — on a 100,000-year schedule.

courses.title: Breaking the 4-Minute Mile
courses.teaser: Doctors said the heart would burst. Then one man did it — and 46 days later, someone else did too.

courses.title: What Miranda Rights Actually Mean
courses.teaser: 'You have the right to remain silent…' — four words from a 1966 case that quietly rewrote every TV arrest scene.

courses.title: Mirror Neurons: The Empathy Cells
courses.teaser: Your brain fires the same neurons when you watch someone eat an apple as when you eat one yourself. Welcome to the strangest neurons we've found.

courses.title: Mitochondrial Eve: Your Oldest Mother
courses.teaser: Every human alive shares one mother — a single woman who lived in Africa about 155,000 years ago. And we can prove it.

courses.title: Modus Ponens: Logic's Bedrock
courses.teaser: The world's shortest, oldest, and most unbreakable argument — taught by the Stoics, trusted by every mathematician alive.

courses.title: Why the Mona Lisa Is So Famous
courses.teaser: She wasn't always the most famous painting in the world. A 1911 heist made her a legend.

courses.title: The Mongol Postal Empire
courses.teaser: A 13th-century message could cross a continent faster than a letter could cross Europe 500 years later.

courses.title: The Monty Hall Problem
courses.teaser: Three doors, one car, one ridiculous math puzzle that broke thousands of PhDs in 1990.

courses.title: Moral Hazard
courses.teaser: Why do people drive more recklessly in rental cars? The answer explains bank bailouts, too.

courses.title: Moral Panic: The Satanic Panic Pattern
courses.teaser: For a decade, America convinced itself that preschool teachers were secret devil worshippers. How?

courses.title: What Muscle Memory Really Is
courses.teaser: Your muscles remember. Literally. Scientists found cells that keep a permanent record of every workout you've ever done.

courses.title: How to Navigate by the Stars
courses.teaser: Phone dead, GPS gone, no compass. Humans crossed oceans using nothing but the night sky. Here's how.

courses.title: Network Effects: Why Winners Take All
courses.teaser: Why does everyone use the same messaging app — even when a better one exists two taps away?

courses.title: How Neural Networks Actually Learn
courses.teaser: They don't 'think.' They adjust millions of tiny knobs until the output stops being wrong.

courses.title: Neuroplasticity: Your Brain Rewires Itself
courses.teaser: Your brain at 60 is not the brain you were born with — and not just because it's older. It's been physically rewiring all along.

courses.title: Norman Doors: Design That Lies to You
courses.teaser: Ever pushed a door that said PULL? It's not you — the door is badly designed. And someone literally wrote the book on it.

courses.title: Ragnarök: The End of the Norse Gods
courses.teaser: What if the gods knew they would die — and fought anyway?

courses.title: When Babies Realize You Still Exist
courses.teaser: Why does peekaboo blow a baby's mind? Because for months, they genuinely think you vanished.

courses.title: Occam's Razor
courses.teaser: A 14th-century friar's rule of thumb quietly runs modern science, detective work, and your everyday decisions.

courses.title: How Ocean Currents Control the Climate
courses.teaser: A slow-motion river in the Atlantic keeps London warmer than Labrador — and it's starting to falter.

courses.title: Why Octopus Eyes Work Better Than Ours
courses.teaser: Octopuses are colorblind — and yet they match their skin to a coral reef. Their eyes have a design trick we don't.

courses.title: How Smart Is an Octopus?
courses.teaser: An octopus has nine brains, three hearts, and can solve puzzles — possibly the closest thing to alien intelligence on Earth.

courses.title: Opportunity Cost: The Hidden Price of Everything
courses.teaser: Every yes is a no. Every hour spent here is an hour not spent there. Economists have a name for that invisible bill.

courses.title: The Osborne Effect
courses.teaser: How a CEO killed his own company by announcing a product too early — and gave the phenomenon a name.

courses.title: Ötzi: The 5,300-Year-Old Murder Victim
courses.teaser: Two hikers found a body in the Alps. It turned out to be a Copper Age man shot in the back — and still bleeding clues.

courses.title: How the Panama Canal's Locks Work
courses.teaser: How do you lift a 100,000-ton ship 85 feet into the air — using nothing but water and gravity?

courses.title: The Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)
courses.teaser: Why 80% of your results come from just 20% of your effort — and how an Italian economist spotted it in pea pods.

courses.title: Parkinson's Law
courses.teaser: A 1-hour task takes an hour. Give yourself a week and somehow — it takes a week. Why?

courses.title: Why Passports Come in Only 4 Colors
courses.teaser: There's no rule. No treaty. Yet almost every passport in the world is red, blue, green, or black.

courses.title: Why '123456' Won't Save You
courses.teaser: Your password isn't judged by how clever it feels — it's judged by how boring it is to a machine making a billion guesses a second.

courses.title: How Pasteur Killed an Ancient Belief
courses.teaser: For 2,000 years everyone knew maggots grew from rotten meat — until a bent glass neck proved them wrong.

courses.title: Why Peacocks Have Useless Tails
courses.teaser: The peacock's tail almost broke Darwin's theory. It doesn't help him fly, hide, or fight. So why did evolution build it?

courses.title: The Accidental Discovery of Penicillin
courses.teaser: A messy lab, a forgotten petri dish, and a draft from an open window — the mistake that saved hundreds of millions of lives.

courses.title: The Great Dying: Earth's Worst Extinction
courses.teaser: 252 million years ago, 90% of all life on Earth vanished. The dinosaurs hadn't even arrived yet — and the planet nearly didn't recover.

courses.title: The Peter Principle
courses.teaser: In every hierarchy, people rise until they reach the job they're bad at — and there they stay.

courses.title: How Phishing Actually Works
courses.teaser: The most devastating cyberattacks don't break software. They break people — with a convincing email and a fake login page.

courses.title: Phonemes: The Sound Atoms of Language
courses.teaser: The difference between 'ship' and 'sip' is a single tiny sound. That sound is a phoneme — and your brain is a phoneme machine.

courses.title: How a Pidgin Becomes a Creole
courses.teaser: Strangers forced to trade invent a broken patchwork language. A generation later, their kids turn it into grammar. How?

courses.title: The Pigeonhole Principle
courses.teaser: If you have 13 pigeons and 12 boxes, at least two pigeons share a box. That obvious fact has surprisingly powerful consequences.

courses.title: The Placebo Effect
courses.teaser: A sugar pill can ease real pain. A saline injection can boost real performance. Your brain, it turns out, is a pharmacy.

courses.title: The Pomodoro Technique
courses.teaser: A 1980s Italian student set a tomato-shaped kitchen timer to 25 minutes — and accidentally invented the most famous focus hack on Earth.

courses.title: How Pompeii Was Frozen in Time
courses.teaser: A city of 20,000 vanished in 18 hours — and was preserved so perfectly that loaves of bread still sit in the ovens.

courses.title: Price Anchoring: Why $9.99 Works
courses.teaser: Your brain doesn't judge price by value — it judges by the first number it saw. Here's how retailers weaponize that.

courses.title: Why Primates Groom Each Other for Hours
courses.teaser: It's not really about cleanliness. Grooming is primate Facebook — and it may have given us language.

courses.title: The Prisoner's Dilemma
courses.teaser: Two suspects, two cells, zero trust — and a puzzle that rewrites how we think about cooperation.

courses.title: Why Purple Became the Color of Royalty
courses.teaser: It took 12,000 rotting sea snails to dye one Roman toga. The color of kings was, for most of history, the color of luxury glue.

courses.title: How the Pyramids Were Built
courses.teaser: Forget aliens — the real story involves beer rations, a papyrus diary, and a flooded canal straight to Giza.

courses.title: Quantum Entanglement
courses.teaser: Two particles, lightyears apart, flip in perfect sync the moment you check one. Einstein hated it. Experiments confirmed it.

courses.title: How Ransomware Holds Files Hostage
courses.teaser: One click. Minutes later, every file on the network is unreadable — and a countdown timer appears.

courses.title: The Red Queen Hypothesis
courses.teaser: In evolution, you have to run as fast as you can just to stay in the same place. And some species can't.

courses.title: Why Roman Concrete Still Stands
courses.teaser: Modern concrete crumbles in 50 years. A Roman seawall has been healing itself for 2,000.

courses.title: Cracking the Rosetta Stone
courses.teaser: One broken slab unlocked 3,000 years of Egyptian silence — and two men raced for 20 years to read it.

courses.title: The Rule of Thirds
courses.teaser: The most famous rule in photography — and the one every great photographer eventually learns to break.

courses.title: Russell's Paradox
courses.teaser: In 1902, a single two-page letter broke the foundations of mathematics. It was addressed politely — but it was a grenade.

courses.title: Does Language Shape How You Think?
courses.teaser: Russians see more blues. The Kuuk Thaayorre always know which way is north. Is your mother tongue secretly rewiring your brain?

courses.title: Why 'Only 3 Left' Works on You
courses.teaser: Your brain treats a red 'almost sold out' label like a threat to your survival. That's not a metaphor.

courses.title: Schrödinger's Cat, Actually Explained
courses.teaser: Schrödinger didn't believe the cat was both alive and dead. He invented it to mock the physicists who did.

courses.title: The Separation of Powers
courses.teaser: One room full of people making, enforcing, and judging the same laws is called a dictatorship. Here's why democracies split the job into three.

courses.title: Servant Leadership
courses.teaser: What if the best bosses don't command from the top — but serve from underneath?

courses.title: Words Shakespeare Invented
courses.teaser: Eyeball. Swagger. Lonely. Assassination. One man gave English over 1,700 new words.

courses.title: The Ship of Theseus
courses.teaser: If you replace every plank of a ship, one at a time, is it still the same ship? And what does that mean for you?

courses.title: Six Degrees of Separation
courses.teaser: A Harvard psychologist mailed 160 letters to random strangers — and discovered that the world is far smaller than it looks.

courses.title: Sleep Cycles Decoded
courses.teaser: Why do you wake up groggy after 9 hours but refreshed after 7? The answer is a 90-minute wave.

courses.title: The Social Contract
courses.teaser: You never signed anything. So why do you have to obey the law?

courses.title: Spaced Repetition: The Learning Hack
courses.teaser: Why cramming guarantees you'll forget — and why quizzing yourself just before you forget makes knowledge almost impossible to lose.

courses.title: Stalemate: The Draw That Shouldn't Exist
courses.teaser: You have a queen, a rook, and twelve pawns. Your opponent has only a king. And you just lost half a point.

courses.title: Why Some Crimes Expire
courses.teaser: Steal a bike in 2015 and confess in 2026 — in many places, the law can't touch you anymore. Why?

courses.title: Steelmanning: Arguing Against Yourself
courses.teaser: The strongest way to win an argument? Make the other side's case better than they can.

courses.title: Stockholm Syndrome
courses.teaser: Six days in a bank vault. Four hostages who defended their captors. The name stuck — but was it ever really real?

courses.title: Stoicism in 5 Minutes
courses.teaser: A Roman emperor wrote a private journal to stop himself from losing his temper. Two thousand years later, it's still the best advice ever written.

courses.title: The Fig That Strangles Trees
courses.teaser: It starts life as a single seed in a bird dropping — and ends it as a killer wearing another tree's ghost.

courses.title: Why Sugar Makes You Crash
courses.teaser: Why does a donut feel amazing for 20 minutes — and then turn you into a zombie? Meet the hormonal rollercoaster.

courses.title: The Survival Rule of Threes
courses.teaser: Three minutes without air. Three hours without shelter. Three days without water. This simple rule has saved more lives than any gear.

courses.title: Survivorship Bias
courses.teaser: The planes that made it back had bullet holes everywhere — except the spots that actually mattered. Here's why you keep making the same mistake.

courses.title: Why T. Rex Had Such Tiny Arms
courses.teaser: The biggest land predator that ever walked had arms you couldn't touch your own face with. Paleontologists have a theory.

courses.title: The Tacoma Narrows Bridge Disaster
courses.teaser: A 40 mph wind. A half-mile bridge. And the most famous engineering failure ever captured on film.

courses.title: Tardigrades: Nature's Toughest Animal
courses.teaser: Boiled, frozen, vacuum-dried, blasted with radiation, launched into space. This half-millimeter animal shrugs it all off.

courses.title: The Terracotta Army
courses.teaser: A farmer digging a well in 1974 put his shovel through the face of a 2,200-year-old soldier.

courses.title: Why the Terrible Twos Are Actually Good
courses.teaser: That tiny dictator throwing spaghetti on the floor? Developmentally, it's one of the most important milestones of their life.

courses.title: Why Theater's Symbol Is Two Masks
courses.teaser: One grins, one weeps — and they've been glued together for 2,500 years. Who are they, really?

courses.title: The Three-Act Structure
courses.teaser: Why does almost every movie you love follow the same hidden shape?

courses.title: The Three Sisters: An Ancient Planting Trick
courses.teaser: Indigenous farmers figured out a way to grow three crops in the same hole — and each one makes the other two stronger.

courses.title: Why You See Lightning Before You Hear Thunder
courses.teaser: Light moves a million times faster than sound. That gap between flash and boom is physics you can count on your fingers.

courses.title: Why Time Slows Near a Black Hole
courses.teaser: If you orbited a black hole for an hour, your friends back home might have aged seven years. Here's why.

courses.title: The Tragedy of the Commons
courses.teaser: Why rational individuals, all doing what's best for themselves, can destroy the thing they depend on.

courses.title: How Trees Talk Through Fungi
courses.teaser: Beneath every old forest is a web of living cables — and the trees use it to send warnings, trade sugar, and feed their young.

courses.title: The Trolley Problem
courses.teaser: One death to save five. Would you pull the lever? Would you push a stranger off a bridge? The difference might reveal how morality actually works.

courses.title: The Turing Test
courses.teaser: In 1950, a mathematician asked: can a machine fool you into thinking it's human? The answer is getting uncomfortable.

courses.title: Why Two-Factor Authentication Matters
courses.teaser: Passwords leak. A lot. Here's the little six-digit trick that stops 99% of account takeovers cold.

courses.title: Umami: The Fifth Taste
courses.teaser: For most of history, chefs swore there were four tastes. Then a Japanese scientist boiled some seaweed and changed cooking forever.

courses.title: The Unreliable Narrator
courses.teaser: What if the person telling you the story is lying — and doesn't know it?

courses.title: Utilitarianism
courses.teaser: One rule: do whatever creates the most happiness. It sounds simple. Then you actually try to live by it.

courses.title: How Vaccines Teach the Immune System
courses.teaser: A vaccine is a flashcard for your immune system — a harmless preview of an enemy it might meet years from now.

courses.title: Vantablack: The Color You Can't Photograph
courses.teaser: It absorbs 99.965% of light — and one artist bought the exclusive right to use it.

courses.title: Rawls's Veil of Ignorance
courses.teaser: Design a society — but you don't get to know who you'll be in it. Suddenly, fairness gets interesting.

courses.title: Venus: Earth's Evil Twin
courses.teaser: Same size as Earth. Same rock. Same water, once. So why is Venus a 465°C hellscape that would crush you and cook you at the same time?

courses.title: The Viking Sunstone
courses.teaser: How did the Vikings navigate without a compass — even when the sun was hidden?

courses.title: VO₂ Max: The Fitness Number
courses.teaser: One number predicts how long you'll live better than blood pressure or cholesterol. Most people have never measured it.

courses.title: Why Wedding Dresses Are White
courses.teaser: For most of human history, brides wore their best dress in any color. Then a 20-year-old queen changed the rules in one afternoon.

courses.title: Whale Fall: How a Dead Whale Feeds a World
courses.teaser: When a whale dies and sinks, its body becomes a tropical island on the abyss floor — hosting creatures that exist nowhere else.

courses.title: Are Viruses Actually Alive?
courses.teaser: They reproduce, they evolve, they outnumber us — but most biologists will tell you viruses aren't alive. Here's why it's complicated.

courses.title: Why Airplane Food Tastes Bland
courses.teaser: It's not the chef. It's your tongue losing 30% of its power at 35,000 feet.

courses.title: Why Airplanes Actually Fly
courses.teaser: The explanation you learned in school is mostly wrong. Here's what really holds 400 tons of metal in the air.

courses.title: Why Elite Athletes Choke
courses.teaser: The better you are, the worse pressure hits you. The brain science behind the yips is strange.

courses.title: Why Babies Cry More at Night
courses.teaser: Your baby's 3 a.m. protest might be evolutionary — and even a little bit sneaky.

courses.title: Why Banksy Shredded His Own Painting
courses.teaser: Sotheby's slammed the hammer at £1.04 million — and the painting immediately began destroying itself.

courses.title: Why Cats Purr (It's Not Just Happiness)
courses.teaser: They purr while being petted — and also while giving birth, while injured, while dying. The real reason is stranger.

courses.title: Why Chess Was Invented as a War Game
courses.teaser: Long before grandmasters, kings played chess to rehearse battles. The board itself is a battlefield.

courses.title: Why Some Chords Sound Sad
courses.teaser: Change one note by a tiny amount and a happy chord collapses into a melancholy one. Here's the physics and culture behind the feeling.

courses.title: What Killed the Dinosaurs
courses.teaser: An asteroid the size of Mount Everest hit Mexico at 20 km/s. Within hours, the sky rained glass. Within a year, 75% of life was gone.

courses.title: Why Geese Fly in V Formation
courses.teaser: They're not just showing off — geese in a V can fly 70% farther than a single bird. Here's the physics behind it.

courses.title: Why Gothic Cathedrals Reach for the Sky
courses.teaser: Medieval builders were racing upward — until Beauvais Cathedral collapsed and physics finally said 'enough.'

courses.title: Why Humans Are the Only Animals That Cry
courses.teaser: Dogs howl. Elephants grieve. But only humans produce tears from feeling. Nobody is quite sure why we do it.

courses.title: Why Humans Gossip
courses.teaser: Gossip isn't a flaw — it's the social technology that let our species dominate the planet.

courses.title: Why Humans Walk on Two Legs
courses.teaser: Standing up cost us speed, stability, and painless childbirth. So why did our ancestors do it?

courses.title: Why Hurricanes Spin
courses.teaser: Hurricanes in the northern hemisphere always spin counterclockwise. Southern ones always spin clockwise. Why?

courses.title: Why Ice Floats
courses.teaser: Almost every solid sinks in its own liquid. Water breaks the rule — and that's why fish survive winter.

courses.title: Why Blue Jeans Are Blue
courses.teaser: The color of every pair of jeans you own traces back to a tropical plant and a tiny chemical quirk.

courses.title: Why Children Won't Stop Asking 'Why?'
courses.teaser: By age 4, your kid will ask about 300 questions a day. It isn't a stall tactic — it's a tiny scientist at work.

courses.title: Why We All Have Lawns
courses.teaser: A uniform green rectangle in front of your house is a 400-year-old status symbol you probably inherited without knowing it.

courses.title: Why Lie Detectors Don't Actually Work
courses.teaser: Sweat. Heart rate. A scribbling needle. The polygraph looks like science — but guessing a coin flip is barely worse.

courses.title: Why Men Stopped Wearing High Heels
courses.teaser: Louis XIV wore four-inch red heels. Cowboys wore them to grip stirrups. So when did heels become a women-only thing?

courses.title: Why Movies Are 24 Frames Per Second
courses.teaser: Why does cinema look like cinema — and video look like soap opera? The answer is an old compromise between film, sound, and cost.

courses.title: Why Onions Make You Cry
courses.teaser: That onion isn't just releasing a smell — it's firing a tiny chemical weapon at your face, on purpose.

courses.title: Why Picasso Painted People Like Boxes
courses.teaser: Picasso could paint like Raphael at 14 — so why did he start making faces look like shattered glass?

courses.title: Why Plants Are Green
courses.teaser: The sun pours out every color — so why do plants throw away the most powerful one?

courses.title: Why 0.999... Equals 1
courses.teaser: 0.999... isn't almost 1. It's exactly 1. And once you see why, you can't unsee it.

courses.title: Why 'Q' Almost Always Needs 'U'
courses.teaser: The strangest rule in English has a 3,000-year journey behind it — through Phoenicia, Rome, and medieval monks.

courses.title: How Rainbows Appear
courses.teaser: Every rainbow is a personal hallucination — made just for your eyes.

courses.title: Why Flash Photos Give You Red Eyes
courses.teaser: That creepy glow in old birthday photos is literally a picture of the inside of your eyeball.

courses.title: The Math of Rock Paper Scissors
courses.teaser: A 'random' game that isn't random — and the reason you probably throw rock when it matters.

courses.title: Why Salt Makes Everything Taste Better
courses.teaser: Salt doesn't just taste salty — it hacks your tongue, your ingredients, and even suppresses bitterness. Here's how.

courses.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
courses.teaser: The sun is yellow-white, space is black. So why is the air above you blue?

courses.title: Why Skyscrapers Sway on Purpose
courses.teaser: The top of the Burj Khalifa can drift almost 2 meters in the wind. That's not a bug — that's the design.

courses.title: Why Every Snowflake Is Different
courses.teaser: Not a myth — and not magic. It's because each flake takes a different elevator ride through the sky.

courses.title: Why Soil Smells After Rain (Petrichor)
courses.teaser: That earthy smell after the first rain? It's a bacterial molecule — and your nose is astonishingly good at catching it.

courses.title: The Wilhelm Scream: Hollywood's Inside Joke
courses.teaser: The same six-second scream has been used in over 400 movies. Once you hear it, you'll hear it forever.

courses.title: Why Electrical Plugs Have Three Prongs
courses.teaser: Two prongs run your toaster. The third one exists for exactly one reason: to keep you alive when something goes wrong.

courses.title: Why Time Zones Are So Weird
courses.teaser: Why is all of China one time zone — and Nepal 45 minutes off from everyone?

courses.title: Why We Crave Salt
courses.teaser: Nations have fought wars over salt. Your body will genuinely change its behavior to find some. Here's the ancient wiring behind the craving.

courses.title: Why We Dream
courses.teaser: You spend about six years of your life dreaming. Nobody fully agrees on why — but the leading theories are wild.

courses.title: Why Fevers Actually Help You
courses.teaser: A fever isn't your body breaking — it's your body fighting. And suppressing it too fast may cost you.

courses.title: Why Lifting Weights Makes You Stronger
courses.teaser: The real reason you get stronger isn't your muscles getting bigger — it's your brain learning a new trick.

courses.title: Why We Laugh
courses.teaser: Laughter isn't really about jokes. It's a 10-million-year-old social signal that predates language itself.

courses.title: Why We Procrastinate
courses.teaser: Procrastination isn't a time-management problem. It's an emotion-management problem — and your brain is actively trying to protect you.

courses.title: Why Swearing Actually Helps
courses.teaser: Cursing reduces pain, boosts strength, and lives in a different brain region than normal speech.

courses.title: Why We Yawn (and Why It's Contagious)
courses.teaser: Reading this sentence just made some of you yawn. Nobody knows exactly why — but we have very good guesses.

courses.title: Why Zebras Are Striped
courses.teaser: Camouflage? Cooling? Confusing lions? Scientists argued for 150 years — and the answer turned out to be biting flies.

courses.title: The Wim Hof Method
courses.teaser: A Dutch extreme athlete claims breathing and ice water can rewire your immune system. Does the science back him up?

courses.title: The Zeigarnik Effect
courses.teaser: Your brain is haunted by the things you started and didn't finish. A Russian psychologist discovered why — in a Vienna café in 1927.

courses.title: How the Zipper Conquered the World
courses.teaser: It took 30 years, two near-bankruptcies, and a sailor's boot to turn a laughable invention into a global standard.

courses.title: Zone 2: The Most Overlooked Cardio
courses.teaser: It feels almost too easy — but this is the pace elite athletes spend most of their training time at. And it might add years to your life.
